q:
Why buoyant force is upward
Which of the following helps explain why the buoyant force exerted on an object submerged in a fluid on Earth is always directed upward?
o: The buoyant force forms a Newton’s third law pair with the force of gravity on the object.
The fluid pressure exerts a force that balances the weight of the object, which means pressure is only exerted upward on the object.
Fluid pressure is exerted on all surfaces of an object and increases with depth. The horizontal forces due to the pressure cancel, but the vertical forces do not.
The surface tension between fluid molecules tends to resist the object moving downward through the surface of the fluid. This resistive force causes a net force upward.

q:
Energy of fluid in pipe
A volume ΔV of an ideal fluid enters the left side of a cylindrical pipe with speed v1 during a time interval Δt, and an equal volume ΔV of ideal fluid exits the right side of the pipe with speed v2 during the same time interval Δt. The cross-sectional areas of the left end and right end of the pipe are A1 and A2 , respectively; the pressures at the left end and right end of the pipe are P1 and P2 , respectively; and the distance between the centers of the two sections is Δh , as shown in the figure. Which equation and description best expresses the motion of the ideal fluid in the cylindrical pipe in terms of conservation of energy?
o: A sub 1, v sub 1, delta t equals, A sub 2 ,v sub 2, delta t . The volume flow rate of the fluid in the pipe must be the same for both sections of pipe. To conserve the volume flow rate of the fluid in the pipe, the fluid velocity must be slower in the left side and faster in the right side.

P sub 1 plus, one half rho v sub 1 squared, equals P sub 2 plus, rho g delta h, plus one half rho v sub 2 squared . The speed of a fluid at two different locations in a pipe depends on the pressure of the fluid at each location and the work done by gravity on the fluid as it moves from one location to the other.

W sub net equals, P sub 1 A sub 1, v sub 1 delta t, minus, P sub 2 A sub 2, v sub 2 delta t . The net work done on the fluid in the pipe is the sum of the work done by the pressure P sub 1 on the volume of fluid A sub 1 v sub 1 delta t at the left end of the pipe plus the negative work done by the pressure P sub 2 on an equal volume of fluid A sub 2 v sub 2 delta t at the right end of the pipe.

one half times rho delta V, times open parenthesis, v sub 2 squared, minus v sub 1 squared, close parenthesis, equals rho g delta h . An ideal fluid will experience no energy loss to nonconservative forces; therefore, any change in the kinetic energy of the fluid between point 1 and point 2 is equal to the change in the gravitational potential energy of the fluid between point 1 and point 2 because mechanical energy is conserved within the fluid in the pipe.

Calculate the gauge pressure in the feeder pipe at this point.
